data.title: American Homes 4 Rent: Solid Results Are Likely To Continue
data.description:
Shares of American Homes 4 Rent (NYSE:AMH) have been a solid performer over the past year, delivering an over 9% total return including its dividend. AMH has benefitted from favorable macro fundamentals, and the company delivered a solid Q1. I last covered shares in December, rating AMH a “hold.” Since then, the stock has returned 4% while the market has declined modestly. Shortly after my article, shares did reach my $35 buy level and have since performed well. While its total return has been consistent with my expectations, its outperformance suggests a buy rating could have been justified from the outset. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit shares.

In the company’s first quarter reported on May 1st, American Homes 4 Rent generated $0.46 of FFO, beating consensus by a penny. This was up 6.6% from last year. Rental revenue rose by 8% to $459 million while core expenses rose by 6.3%, making AMH one of the few landlords to see margin expansion over the past year. EBITDA rose by nearly 9% to $231 million as margins expanded by 30bps to 57.9%. FFO grew more slowly than EBITDA, given a 1% increase in the share count and a ~15% rise in interest expense to $45 million.

AMH is one of the leading single-family rental companies. While multifamily REITs are a long-standing concept, prior to the financial crisis, single-family rentals were largely done on a smaller scale, but firms like AMH have institutionalized the asset class. As you can see below, AMH operates across much of the country, but it is primarily focused in growth markets across the Sun Belt. AMH now owns over 60,000 properties.

I continue to view the macro environment as favorable for the asset class. AMH ultimately serves consumers who prefer the single-family residential lifestyle to apartment living, typically young families, but who cannot afford or do not want to buy a home. With millennials entering household formation years, demographics are favorable. Plus with home prices high and interest rates elevated, home affordability is very constrained, making renting an attractive option. This should support ongoing demand for its rental properties as well as lengthen tenancy. Indeed, retention is running over 70%.

This strength is evident in recent results. AMH properties had total occupancy of 94.8%, which was up 60bps sequentially. Within its same-home comparable portfolio, occupancy was a strong 95.9%. Same-home net operating income rose by 4.4% from last year with revenue up 4.3% and expenses up 4.2%. I would note that turnover expenses were elevated as it shifts leases to expire in H1 to better align with peak leasing season. With more leases now expiring during the time consumers are looking to lease, future releasing trends should be even stronger.

As it is, rental conditions were favorable. Overall, lease rates rose by 3.6%, including 1.4% on new leases and 4.5% on renewals. Renewal inflation tends to be stronger, as some of its leases are still below market levels. Plus, given the cost of moving, some tenants will choose to stay in a lease even if it is not the best priced option whereas new tenants will isolate to price, since they are already moving. The strong Q1 trend continued into Q2. In April, occupancy is up to 96.3% with over 4% blended lease growth. As you can see, AMH has significantly outperformed multifamily leasing, which is basically flat over the past year, reflecting the fact that the single-family market is distinct.

That said, there have been some pockets of softness in places where construction levels have been elevated. AMH’s biggest markets are seeing slow rent growth with Atlanta, Jacksonville, and Orlando all running below 2.5%. Importantly, new construction activity has come down, which should reduce the supply of new housing and alleviate this pressure. As a result, I expect to see some firming later in the year. Along these lines on the earnings call, management did note some pressures from increased supply in San Antonio, Tampa, and elsewhere, though those pressures appear to be peaking.

AMH also continues to selectively expand its portfolio. It now has 57,900 occupied homes, up over 3% from last year as a result of acquisitions. AMH is also building new homes explicitly to rent, allowing it to negotiate bulk discounts with homebuilders. It did note that tariffs could raise construction costs by 2-3%, but I view this impact as largely manageable. AMH aims to build or buy 1,800-2,000 properties this year for $0.8-$1 billion. This supports ~2-3% underlying growth and implies a cost of $450-500k per property.

Alongside this, it is always rationalizing its portfolio. During the quarter, AMH sold 416 properties for $135 million, or $325k per property. This is in keeping with the company’s strategy of gradually upscaling offerings and maintaining a fresh inventory of homes for rent to maximize occupancy. These dispositions also help to fund acquisitions while maintaining a strong balance sheet. INVH’s $5 billion of debt represents just 23.6% of its total capitalization. This debt carries a 4.5% average interest rate and a 10.3 year average maturity, greatly reducing its sensitivity to short-term rates.

Alongside Q1 results, AMH reiterated guidance. Back in December, I was targeting $1.83-$1.89 in FFO in 2025. Given Q1 results and a solid start to Q2, I view guidance as slightly conservative, though the effort to move lease end-dates to H1 of the year has led to modestly more one-time expenses this year. As such, I see results coming at $1.82-$1.88 now. Ultimately, AMH is delivering in-line with my expectations.

Currently, shares are trading with a 4.8% FFO yield and a 3.1% dividend yield. AMH has over 1.5x dividend coverage, so I view its payout as very secure and likely to rise mid-single-digits over time, given ~2.5-3% rental inflation and 2-3% unit growth. That points to an 8+% long-term return profile. AMH also appears attractive on a net asset value basis.

At its current enterprise value, AMH is being valued at ~$350k/house. Given it sold less desirable homes for $325k and is acquiring new ones above $450k, this is a fairly conservative valuation. Its housing portfolio is being value at a ~7.7% rental yield, given a monthly average rent of $2,250, which is fairly cheap. I generally view ~7% as fair value, and this analysis would suggest a fair value in the $43 area.

On a dividend yield & FFO basis, I view ~$38 as fair value while on a home price NAV basis I view $43 as fair value. Blending the two approaches, I expect AMH shares to move to about $40 within the next year. With modest capital appreciation, a solid dividend, and ongoing dividend growth, I view AMH as an attractive hold, though I do not see enough upside to merit an upgrade to buy, absent a move lower in interest rates.

data.title: Vista Energy: Growth, EBITDA, And Proven Reserves Will Pave The Way
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I grant Vista Energy (NYSE:VIST) a Buy rating. The company has an excellent financial position. It has an 80.18% gross profit margin and a 62.08% EBITDA margin. With these excellent results, the company has allocated significant financial resources to expand its operations. To grant context, its production increased at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 19.09% between 2019 and 2024. Meanwhile, so far this year, its production has increased by 73.13% compared to 2024. This impressive jump was due to capital expenditure and the acquisition of Petronas E&P Argentina S.A. (PEPASA). Furthermore, the company has significant proven resources. With the acquisition of PEPASA, its proven reserves of oil and gas will reach approximately 515.2 million barrels of oil equivalent ((MMboe)), or a 37.31% increase.

I project that the EV/EBITDA ratio of VIST will be 3.24 for 2027, indicating a 10.62% decline compared to its current figure. To implement this projection, I assume lower oil prices and a lower EBITDA-to-revenues ratio compared to values obtained in 2024. In addition, I was conservative with VIST's production forecast for 2026 and 2027. Despite these moderate calculations, my estimation suggests that the company's EV/EBITDA will decline, which is a positive aspect. VIST's high debt level is a risk factor that investors should consider. The company has a 108.36% debt-to-equity ratio. In addition, Argentina has faced episodes of financial crisis, in which the country has experienced currency control and high inflation, among other problems. This scenario could occur again in the future. If these events occur, VIST will be negatively affected. When pondering all the positive and negative aspects of VIST, I think that the positive aspects outweigh the negative ones. Consequently, I grant VIST a buy rating.

Introduction

VIST is a Mexican company headquartered in Mexico City, Mexico, with most of its operations conducted in Argentina. The company is the second-largest oil producer in Vaca Muerta, an important oil location in the South American nation. In fact, in 2024, Argentina accounted for 97.41% of VIST's total net proved reserves, while Mexico represented the remaining 2.59%, as depicted in the first chart below. Oil is the main source of income for the company, representing 86% of the production in 2024. Meanwhile, natural gas accounted for the remaining 14%, as depicted in the second chart below.

In 2018, VIST acquired two energy firms, namely Pampa Energía S.A. and Pluspetrol Resources Corporation. With this operation, it became the fifth-largest oil company in Argentina. The assets acquired were largely in the Neuquina basin. Due to this transaction, the company added 55.7 MMboe to its reserves and operations for 27.47 thousand barrels of oil equivalents per day ((Mboe/d)).

Moving to 2024, the company reported reserves of 242.3 MMboe located in Bajada del Palo Oeste and 73.4 MMboe in Bajada del Palo Este; both locations are in Vaca Muerta. These two blocks accounted for 84.14% of VIST's net proved reserves in that year. To know how the company obtained those reserves, it is necessary to return to 2018. In that year, the Province of Neuquén granted a concession of both blocks to VIST for 35 years.

The next relevant move of VIST was in April 2025. In that moment, the company acquired PEPASA. With this acquisition, the company holds the right of 50% working interest in La Amarga Chica, Vaca Muerta. The remaining 50% working interest is held by YPF S.A. (YPF). In addition, this purchase will add 140 MMboe proven reserves to VIST ((280 MMboe, which equates to 100% working capital)). In 2024, the company had 375.2 MMboe of proven reserves. Consequently, Vista Energy's new reserves will be approximately 515.2 MMboe.

Oil Outlook

This section conducts an analysis to project oil prices. 86% of VIST's production is composed of oil. Consequently, a forecast of fossil fuel energy prices is necessary to project the company's revenues. Energy prices are volatile and are difficult to forecast because they are inelastic to short-term supply or demand disruptions. As such, these forecasts will not necessarily correspond with future realizations.

I will use the WTI forward curve as a tool to forecast oil prices because it shows future prices at which oil producers and oil consumers are hedging their operations. The following chart indicates that buyers and sellers of oil are hedging their operations at prices in the range of $59 per barrel and $62 per barrel. Consequently, I think that assuming a WTI oil price of $60 per barrel for the upcoming years is a reasonable assumption. In my opinion, $2 above or below $60 will not significantly alter VIST's financial performance. In 2024, the WTI average spot oil price was $76.63 per barrel. Therefore, my estimation suggests a decline of 21.70% for the WTI oil price in 2025.

Production

This section discusses VIST's growth outlook. To start with, in September 2023, VIST projected to produce 100 Mboe/d and 150 Mboe/d in 2026 and 2030, respectively. To grant context, in the first half of 2023, the company produced approximately 50 Mboe/d. Consequently, VIST would have to increase its production by 100% to reach its goal set for 2026. However, it achieved this goal early, in April of 2025.

Capital expenditure ((capex)) and asset acquisition have fueled VIST's growth. In 2023-2024, VIST allocated $1,740.97 million in capex. To provide context, in 2022, it registered assets of $2,037.98 million. In other words, in 2023-2024, VIST's capex was 85.43% of its total assets reported in 2022. Due to this significant investment, VIST produced 80.9 Mboe/d in the first quarter of this year. In addition, as commented in the Introduction section, VIST acquired PEPASA in April of this year, adding 39.77 Mboe/d ((50% working capital)). Consequently, when adding the daily production reached in 2025Q1 plus the PEPASA production, VIST's production is 120.67 Mboe/d, which has surpassed its expected production for 2026. Furthermore, the company said that the acquisition will accelerate its goal to reach 150 Mboe/d in 2030.

I think the aforementioned comments provide the input necessary for forecasting VIST's production for 2025 to 2027. I will assume the company will produce 120.67 Mboe/d this year, as depicted in the following table. The company has already achieved this number in April. In addition, it will most likely increase its production in the upcoming months. For instance, in 2025Q1, its capex was $268.5 million. Consequently, in my opinion, my projection is a reasonable assumption.

I now need to explain my projections for 2026 and 2027. In the first quarter of 2025, the company produced 80.9 Mboe/d. Meanwhile, VIST projected to produce 100 Mboe/d in 2026. Therefore, from 2025Q1 to 2026, it has to increase its production by 19.1 Mboe/d to reach its 2026 goal (excluding PEPASA acquisition). I think that assuming that VIST will increase its production by 9.55 Mboe (half of 19.1 Mboe/d) in 2026 and an equal increase in 2027 are reasonable assumptions. In percentage terms, these increases will be 12.55% and 11.15% in 2026 and 2027, respectively. In my opinion, the last two figures are conservative, but I prefer to be conservative rather than optimistic. In the previous section, I assumed a $60 WTI oil price for 2026-2027, which is lower than the $76.63 WTI average oil price of 2024. As such, this decline could reduce VIST’s capex, curbing its production growth rate.

Revenues and EBITDA Outlook

In the previous two sections, I discussed the outlook of the WTI oil price and VIST's production. This section integrates those outlooks to forecast VIST's revenues and EBITDA. Ultimately, I forecast the EV/EBITDA ratio up to 2027, with the scope to draw a conclusion regarding whether the company is an excellent investment opportunity or not.

The following table shows my forecasts discussed in the previous two sections. With these values, I project VIST's revenues for 2025-2027. The procedure was to add the WTI oil price growth forecast and Vista Energy's production growth forecast. For instance, in 2025, I project that VIST's revenues will grow by 51.42%, which is -21.70% + 73.13% = 51.42%.

With the forecast of VIST's revenue growth rates, I projected its revenues, as depicted in the table below. My projection suggests that the revenues will be $3,122 million by 2027, representing an 89.45% increase compared to 2024. For forecasting the EBITDA, I calculated the EBITDA-to-revenues ratio, which is 59.06%, between 2020 and 2024. In 2024, this ratio was 64.52%. Consequently, my projection assumes that the EBITDA will decline as a percentage of the revenues. In this year, the WTI oil price has declined. Therefore, the oil price drop will likely impact VIST's EBITDA. Despite this negative aspect, my estimation suggests that the EBITDA will increase by 11.88% in 2027 compared to 2024.

Up to this point, I have discussed the elements necessary to forecast VIST's EV/EBITDA. The projection will enable me to draw a conclusion. For some readers, this section might be technical. I have explained detail after detail. However, these details make the difference and offer another perspective. I assume that the enterprise value (EV) will remain constant up to 2027. For 2025, my projection indicates a 4.09 EV/EBITDA ratio. Meanwhile, the forecast suggests that this ratio will drop to 3.24 in 2027.

It is time to draw a conclusion using the elements presented in this section. I think that VIST is an appealing investment opportunity. I am assuming that oil prices will decline compared to 2024. In addition, my forecast expects a lower EBITDA-to-revenues ratio in the upcoming years. Furthermore, I expect a 12.55% and 11.15% production growth rate for VIST in 2026 and 2027, respectively. These rates are considerably lower than the 19.09% CAGR of VIST's production between 2019 and 2024. Overall, I have assumed a pessimistic scenario for the company. Despite these headwinds, the EV/EBITDA projected for 2027 is expected to decline by 10.62%, which is a positive aspect.

Peer Analysis

In this section, I compare VIST with other energy companies. YPF and Pampa Energía S.A. (PAM) have fossil energy operations in Vaca Muerta, Argentina. Chevron (CVX) and Exxon Mobil (XOM) have international operations, with presence in Vaca Muerta. I added EOG Resources (EOG), Occidental Petroleum (OXY), and Diamondback Energy (FANG) to compare VIST with American oil companies. The following table shows the ratios to analyze in this section.

The primary strength of VIST is its high growth profit and EBITDA margins, which are 80.18% and 62.08%, respectively. Due to these margins, the company has financial resources to reinvest in its operations. In fact, in the last five years, the revenues increased at a 34.93% CAGR, which is the second-highest value among its peers. I think that the company will continue to grow at a higher rate than the median in the peer group in the upcoming years. Another positive aspect is that VIST has the third-lowest P/E and price-to-cash-flow ratios. Companies with positive growth perspectives and profit margins tend to be overvalued, which is not the case with VIST.

The debt level registered by VIST is a negative aspect. The company has a 108.36% debt-to-equity ratio, the highest value among its peers. In addition, it has a 0.91 current ratio, suggesting that the company might encounter difficulties in paying its short-term liabilities. However, growth and debt go hand in hand. The firm has expanded its operations considerably. As such, the company has borrowed financial resources to achieve its high growth reported in the previous years.

Due to the excellent results that VIST has achieved, the company's stock price has increased by 1,294.40% in the last five years, which is the highest return among its peers. Overall, I think that VIST is an excellent company. The profit and growth ratios suggest that the firm will have a bright future.

Competitive Advantage

VIST has maintained excellent relationships with different Argentinian governments. It set foot in the South American nation in 2018, while Mauricio Macri was the president. Between December 2019 and December 2023, Alberto Fernandez was the president of Argentina, a left-leaning politician. During that period, VIST increased its production by approximately 75.60%. Since December 2023, Javier Milei has been the president of the South American nation, a right-leaning libertarian politician. From December 2023 to 2025Q1, VIST has increased its production by approximately 58.32%. As such, I think VIST will likely not encounter problems with future Argentinian governments.

From a regional perspective, VIST also has excellent relationships with the governors of the Province of Neuquen. That region is where the company has most of its operations. In 2018, the governor of that province granted the concessions of Bajada del Palo Oeste and Bajada del Palo Este ((Vaca Muerta)) to VIST, which I discussed in the Introduction section.

In 2023, the Province of Neuquen announced that it granted concessions on 33% of the total land within Vaca Muerta. For clarity, I have added the link in Spanish. Consequently, there is more land for concessions that the province could grant to more oil and gas companies. In my view, VIST may benefit from this policy in the future. The company has considerably increased its production. As such, the Province of Neuquen may grant more land to the company.

Risk

Argentina has experienced several economic crises throughout its history. It has suffered periods of high inflation and currency control. In addition, it has defaulted its foreign debt on several occasions. While it is true that the current Argentinian president has implemented a more pro-market approach, the country still faces economic problems. For this year, the International Monetary Fund (IFM) projects that Argentina will have an inflation of 35.8% annually, which is considerably high. In addition, there are signs that the local currency is overvalued. If Argentina cannot fix its economy, VIST will be negatively impacted by that economic context.

In 2012, the Argentinian government nationalized YPF, the largest oil producer in that country. There is the risk that a less pro-market Argentinian government will nationalize VIST in the future.

Conclusion

In light of the analysis presented in this article, I consider VIST to be an excellent investment opportunity. The company has an 80.18% gross profit margin and a 62.08% EBITDA margin. Due to these excellent figures, the company has allocated significant resources to expand its operations in Argentina. Between 2016 and 2024, its production increased at a 19.09% CAGR. Meanwhile, this year, the company has also increased its production. In 2024Q1, its production increased by 16.07% compared to 2024. In addition, VIST acquired PEPASA in April of this year, adding 39.77 Mboe/d. With this acquisition, its production increased by 73.13% compared to 2025. As such, this year will likely be excellent for the company.

My estimations suggest that VIST's revenues will increase by 88.45% between 2024 and 2027. Meanwhile, I project that the EBITDA will grow by 11.88% in the same period. I assume lower oil prices and a lower EBITDA-to-revenues ratio for 2025-2027. In addition, I assume that VIST's revenues will grow by 12.55% and 11.15% in 2026 and 2027, respectively. These rates are considerably lower than VIST's production CAGR between 2019 and 2024. Despite these negative scenarios, my estimation indicates that the company's EV/EBITDA will decline by 10.62% in 2027 compared to its current value. When taking all of these factors into consideration, I grant VIST a buy rating.

data.title: Porch Group: The Inflection Is Real, And I'm Staying Long
data.description:
Thesis

Porch Group, Inc. (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:PRCH) started out as a tech company built around home services. Now it’s repositioned itself as a data-heavy, margin-focused platform operating across insurance, software, and consumer engagement. It’s less about services now and more about trying to own the whole homeownership pipeline.

Back in March, I changed my rating on Porch to a "Buy," largely because there was a noticeable and, to my mind, convincing improvement in the company’s fundamentals. They had finally crossed an important line into profitability, reporting record adjusted EBITDA for the fourth quarter of 2024 and setting expectations for further gains through 2025 and beyond. What shifted my view, really, was the company’s move to a higher-margin, fee-based model. It not only improved gross and EBITDA margins but also helped reduce underwriting risk, which had long been one of its more persistent problems.

At the time, I did note some reasonable concerns, mainly around the company’s debt levels and limited revenue visibility, especially given the ongoing weakness in the housing market. Even so, the overall direction, improved earnings, a more streamlined operation, and lower volatility, seemed to support a cautiously optimistic view.

Since then, PRCH has risen by nearly 60% [and considerably more based on the YTD and 6 month data], which has gone some way toward validating that shift in outlook. With the release of Q1 2025 earnings just last week, Porch has now offered its strongest indication yet that the new business model is not only taking effect but also helping to build a more scalable and stable platform, one that the market, for now, still appears to be mispricing.

Porch's Headline Financial Results

Starting with the headline figures, gross profit came in at $69.1 million, up nicely, a 86% year-over-year from Q1 2024. The driver was a 82% gross margin, an almost unheard-of level for a company that only a year ago was struggling with insurance underwriting losses. Adjusted EBITDA reached a quarterly record of $16.9 million, marking a $33.6 million year-over-year improvement. That translates to a 20% adjusted EBITDA margin for the quarter. Net income attributable to Porch shareholders flipped to a positive $8 million. And in a particularly telling sign of operational maturity, cash flow from operations totaled $27 million, up from a negative figure the prior year, and included a $7 million cash collection from the Vesttoo bankruptcy proceedings. As Matt Ehrlichman, CEO and founder, put it on the call, “..we not only generated positive cash for Porch shareholders but significantly so...”

The main driver behind these results is Porch’s shift to a fee-based insurance model, rolled out with the launch of the Porch Insurance Reciprocal Exchange [PIRE] on January 1. By offloading its old carrier, Homeowners of America, into the Reciprocal, Porch has effectively stepped out of the business of gambling on weather. It’s a risk trade: less exposure, more predictable margins. Now, revenue comes from a blend of management fees, policy fees, reinsurance, lead generation, and interest on a 15% surplus note worth $106 million.

Q1 was the first full outing under Porch’s new structure, and it didn’t go badly at all.

The company reported $97 million in Reciprocal written premium, which is about 10% more than the same time last year. More to the point, they managed to turn 50% of that into actual revenue, up from 40%, which came out to $49.8 million in Insurance Services revenue. Profitability at the segment level was $42.3 million in gross profit on an 85% margin, and $25.8 million in adjusted EBITDA at a 52% margin. What it all seems to suggest is that this model, unlikely as it may have sounded at the outset, actually scales rather well as every new dollar of RWP converts into high-margin, recurring income.

Software & Data

That said, Porch didn’t rest on its insurance business alone. The Software & Data segment generated $22 million in revenue, up 4% from Q1 2024. even against a weak housing backdrop, where existing home sales were down 2% year-over-year. Gross profit for the segment was $16.5 million, 75% gross margin, and adjusted EBITDA was $4.6 million, up $2 million year-over-year.

New products were a big part of the story. Floify rolled out “Quick Apply,” a feature that auto-fills up to 80% of mortgage applications, cutting down on friction and speeding up onboarding. Rynoh pushed through a 20% price increase, and the company widened its reach through a new partnership with a major national inspection franchise.

Consumer Services, the smallest of Porch’s three segments, brought in $14.7 million in revenue, down 9% from a year ago. The drop came after shutting down lower-margin businesses like corporate relocation in Q3 2024. Even so, the segment still pulled in $12.2 million in gross profit on an 83% margin.

The adjusted EBITDA loss was $700,000, down $2.2 million from the prior year, as the company redirected spending toward future initiatives and, to paraphrase, Ehrlichman framed it as a deliberate trade-off, explaining they’re investing more aggressively across these businesses to drive faster growth in 2026 and beyond.

The segment results helped push the outlook higher than expected. Porch raised its full-year 2025 revenue guidance to between $400 million and $420 million, $10 million more than the last estimate. Gross profit is now projected at $320 million to $335 million, with margins holding steady at around 80%. Adjusted EBITDA is now projected to land between $60 million and $70 million, up $5 million from earlier guidance. Management says this includes a mid-single-digit hit from new tariffs and bakes in another $5 million in spending for 2025 to scale operations.

Strengthened Capital Base

On the capital side, Porch ended Q1 with $114 million in cash and investments. The Reciprocal is sitting on $198 million in surplus plus non-admitted assets, its highest level to date, and $105 million in statutory surplus. The April 1 reinsurance renewals came with better terms and lower costs. The reinsurance panel now includes more than 40 A-rated partners, which makes the whole setup look more stable on paper.

Porch’s core insurance strategy is focused on high-conversion segments. Homebuyers, for example, account for 40% of all homeowner insurance purchases, and Porch has made inroads with this demographic. Ehrlichman said, “We want to be known as being the best homeowner insurance company for homebuyers.”

That strategy is starting to show results.

In Q1, new business premium more than doubled from a year ago. The company wrote 36,000 policies and expects to top 50,000 in Q2, helped by seasonality and more agent-driven sales. The average premium per policy was $2,683, and most ZIP codes in key states are now back open for business.

Structurally, Porch’s long-term outlook has turned more bullish. The company now says that at full scale, $3 billion in written premium would translate to $2.3 billion in revenue, up from the previous $1.8 billion, and generate $660 million in adjusted EBITDA at a 30% margin.

Valuation and Growth Potential

From a valuation lens, the optics are misleading, if not outright deceptive.

The company’s blended P/E ratio stands at -41.88x, a figure that reflects prior GAAP net losses rather than forward-looking cash generation capacity. In truth, this number is almost useless; it’s a relic of an old accounting framework that no longer applies to the company’s operating reality. Porch is not valued like a recurring revenue software company, nor like a traditional insurer. It sits awkwardly in between, in a space the market doesn’t quite know how to price. But therein lies the opportunity.

Since Porch is now moving away from the risks tied to the insurance claims cycle by switching to a fee-based model, gross written premiums aren’t counted as revenue anymore. Instead, the company gets recurring, high-margin income from policy fees, management fees, surplus note interest, and marketing commissions. It’s a structure designed for both stability and scale. The implications are profound.

This shift not only reduces earnings volatility, it amplifies margin expansion, and that’s precisely what the numbers are beginning to reflect. Free cash flow per share is projected to grow 33.94% year-over-year, more than twice the 15.16% sector median, earning the company a B+ grade on growth under that metric.

The Q1 2025 results mark the first real proof-of-concept. Porch generated that $27 million in operating cash flow in the quarter I brought up earlier, so the trend, at least for now, is finally pointing in the right direction. Operating cash flow for 2025 is projected to grow 13.8%, almost exactly in line with the Information Technology sector median of 13.43%. After some years, here and there, of erratic, boom-and-bust cash flow, this kind of stability matters.

What’s getting missed here is that Porch isn’t chasing top-line growth just to show growth anymore. GAAP revenue dropped 6.81% year-over-year and fell another 9.27% this past quarter to $104.75 million, even though the company beat consensus by more than $25 million.

Most of that decline comes from a change in how revenue is recognized, not an actual drop in demand. But the headline number still looks bad, and that carries its own kind of risk.

Forward revenue growth is projected at just 2.94%, well behind the sector median of 6.82%. So what you have is a company that, on paper, looks like it’s shrinking, even as the actual quality of its revenue and margins is clearly getting better.

Macroeconomic Headwinds

Still, challenges abound, and the transformation story is far from complete. The housing market, core to Porch’s Software & Data and Consumer Services segments, remains in a state of inertia. CEO Matt Ehrlichman was explicit in the Q1 call: “We remain cautious and are assuming a flat housing market for the year.” That assumption puts pressure on two key revenue streams tied to home transactions: still stuck dealing with affordability problems and low inventory turnover. In practical terms, it means organic growth in those areas will likely stay flat or decline, even if the rest of the business holds steady.

Insurance premium growth is happening, but the way it’s happening matters. A big chunk of it is coming from price hikes, not new customers. In Texas alone, Porch pushed through a 16% rate increase. That props up revenue in the short term, but it’s not a strategy you can keep leaning on forever. Eventually, people start walking, and demand elasticity becomes a real problem.

The company’s also just getting started when it comes to becoming a primary carrier for agents. COO Sean Neagle put it bluntly: “We are very early days.” It’s an honest admission, and it highlights the bigger issue, none of this works without execution. The company’s already warning that Q2 adjusted EBITDA will be $5 million to $7 million lower than Q1, mostly because of changes in its reinsurance setup. They’re expecting a rebound in Q3 and Q4, but near-term visibility isn’t great, and that uncertainty could be enough to keep some investors on the fence.

Final Takeaway

Porch is making some progress on the balance sheet, but the debt load is still a problem. The company’s market cap sits at $1.08 billion [per FAST Graphs data above], while enterprise value is $1.38 billion, meaning there’s still a lot of debt in the system. Long-term debt-to-capital is 114.69%, which is high even by tech growth standards. That kind of leverage limits flexibility and makes flawless execution a requirement, not a nice-to-have.

Still, looking ahead, as I noted earlier, Porch’s long-term plan is built around reaching $3 billion in written premium through the Reciprocal and converting half of that into revenue. If it works, that would mean over $2 billion in recurring, higher-margin income and potentially hundreds of millions in annual EBITDA.

All things considered, if things keep going this way, Porch may eventually shake off its small-cap anonymity and start resembling a proper software-enabled financial services outfit. Yes, there are still issues, but the turning point seems genuine, the plan appears to be working, and the market doesn’t quite seem to have caught on yet. I’m sticking with a Buy.

data.title: Alphabet: A Profitable Strategy Even With A Slightly Down Stock Price
data.description:
Article Thesis

I first covered Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOGL, GOOG) in November last year, with the stock at ~$164 and my base-case Fair Value estimate at $198 derived from a DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) model and backed by a comparison with peers, or with Google’s 5-year averages. I rated the stock as a “Buy” due to this slight undervaluation combined with an overall strong economic moat:

  • Google Search ads: strong moat
  • "Other" properties (Google Mail, Google Maps, Google Play): strong moat
  • YouTube ads: strong moat
  • Google Cloud: strong moat
  • Google subscriptions (YouTube, Google Pixel): narrow moat
  • Google network: between no-moat and narrow moat, but that’s only about ~9% of revenues.

However, I rated it only as a “Hold” in February, with the stock a little above $185. It was in the Fair Value zone and with short-term pressures due to the U.S. Department of Justice (DOJ) accusations of monopolistic practices, combined with some mid-term pressure over Google Search from the AI engines. I also trimmed my position in the meantime, but more about strategy later.

Today, we have another extra headwind, the macro pressure coming from the trade war with possible tariffs and/or recession. But the stock is even a little lower that it was in November. Isn’t that enough to place it in the “Buy” zone again?

Last Quarter Highlights

Management emphasized again progress in AI infrastructure, with achievements such as:

  • Ironwood, a TPU (Tensor Processing Unit) designed for inference at scale.
  • Gemini 2.5 Pro and Gemini 2.5 Flash, for a balance between quality and cost.
  • The open model Gemma 3.
  • AI Overviews, with more than 1.5 billion users every month.
  • Agent Development Kit, for building sophisticated AI agents.
  • Waymo, servicing 5x more passengers than a year ago.

Revenue for Q1 grew 12% year over year, but Google Cloud had a growth of “only” 28%, lower than Microsoft Azure. However, this is due to temporary capacity problems, the company planning to address the situation later this year.

It seems that macro factors are leaving their mark on analysts' estimates, because we can see some downward revisions since February, and I agree with that. And the more distant into the future, the higher the impact, which is normal again, due to the compound effect:

In terms of profitability, they had about 58.5% gross margin last year, and I continue to believe this will slowly grow higher, with less exposure to ads and more exposure to Google Cloud and AI.

While the operating margin had a slight pullback due to the high R&D expenses related to so many AI projects, I keep my opinion that they will benefit from scale, bringing the operating margin from about 33% to ~36% in 2030 and to ~38% in 2034.

Free cash flow might be a bit lower in 2025 than it used to be, due to huge $75B CapEx. However, I expect this very elevated level for one-two years, than CapEx should ease a little bit as a percentage of sales.

Besides, with ~$95B in cash & equivalents and only ~$11B debt, they can afford not only this CapEx, but also to easily pay their dividend (although we saw a lower dividend growth rate than I expected), to continue their share buybacks, and to go for substantial acquisitions, like the security platform Wiz.

Fair Value

For a relative valuation, let’s see a comparison with Alphabet’s five-year averages:

  • PEG: probably the most useful multiple for this type of companies with a blend of profitability and growth; with a value of 1.07, compared to 1.43 5Y average, this implies about 33% undervaluation.
  • EV / EBITDA paints about the same idea.
  • EV / Sales, however, gives only about 18% undervaluation.

For an absolute valuation, I built a DCF model in three stages:

  • For Stage I (2025-2029) I use the consensus revenue estimates (see above section), Operating Margin gradually up to 35.5% in 2029 and peak CapEx margin in 2025.
  • For Stage II (2030-2034), I model a gradual moderation in growth from 8% in 2030 to 5% in 2034, and a terminal operating margin of 38% (see above section).
  • For Stage III, I model 3% growth in perpetuity (compared to 2% in my initial model and 2.5% in February), a slight increase based on several factors: potential of their internally designed AI chips as well as other AI technologies, potential in cyber-security, and “mind-boggling” potential of its quantum computing chip.

I use a WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital) of 9.25%, slightly higher than the previous 9.1%, due to increased risks from tariffs and a possible recession (more about risks later).

With these assumptions, Fair Value estimate is $193.4, very slightly lower than $198 that I estimated in November, or than $205 from February, due to downward sales revisions for the next five years and an increased WACC, partially offset by a higher growth in perpetuity.

That means about 23% potential upside from the current price ($157), which is backed by the relative valuation. While for other companies I am looking for a more significant undervaluation, I think that Alphabet deserves a “Buy” rating, considering a combination of ~10% growth ahead, some operational leverage and a strong economic moat for most of its business segments.

Risks

I talked about increased macro risks, which made me increase WACC. While Alphabet might seem insulated from the trade war, I think that is not quite clear. First, there is a possibility that Google Cloud might be a bit affected if their customers decide to “wait and see”. Then, if a recession will hit, ads will be naturally affected. Finally, depending on the magnitude of U.S. tariffs, Europe might retaliate with a digital services tax, and Google could be a target.

In my initial article, I discussed in detail the DOJ proposed remedies against Google's allegedly monopolistic practices. I don't think all of them will be accepted in court, and Google could appeal, but in the unlikely event that all those proposals will be implemented, I estimated about 9%-10% impact, which will give us a Fair Value of ~$175.

Google Search is, by far, their largest segment. Therefore, when an Apple exec expressed Search concerns last week, that was enough to send the stock down by about 9%. But Google issued a rebuke, and let’s take a look, Google’s market share dropped from 90.9% to 89.65% in the last year:

If this trend will continue, with 1%-2% lower market share per year, Google Search will still be the most used search engine in 10 years from now, so I think it’s a long time until we’ll see advertisers fleeing en masse. Besides, Google is not a passive observer, they are an active player in the AI field. In my initial article I also modeled a pessimistic scenario, with a serious drop in Google Search ads after 2029, and this would affect Fair Value by another ~$10, which would give us $165.

Strategy

My strategy here was (and is) twofold: a stock position and a bull Put Spread position with options.

Overall position size:

As mentioned with other occasions, the weight of a position in my portfolio is given by my estimates for rating and economic moat:

Strong Buy

…where 1x = a normal weight position.

For Alphabet, with a strong moat and a “Buy” rating in November, it was a 1.5x position, shares and bull Put Spread. Immediately after the stock crossed my Fair Value at that time (at ~$199) I trimmed my overall position, by selling some shares, then by closing the Put Spread which was deeply profitable. However, at $185, I began to modestly increase my position weight again through Put Spreads (more details below).

My overall position is only about 1x today, although I target 1.5x for a “Strong moat” in the “Buy” zone. However, these are not normal times, like I described in the Risks section, so I keep my overall position a bit lower than normal.

Strategy #1: Stock position

Although options can provide significantly higher leverage for certain stock price ranges, I had half of my Google’s position in November through shares, with the stock at ~$164. Back then, a higher growth was at the horizon instead of higher tariffs, so I also wanted to have some potentially unlimited upside exposure.

However, like I explained in my article from February, I sold part of the shares when the stock exceeded my Fair Value estimate for that time, at $199.3, for about 21% in two months. I always trim my positions if they go above my Fair Value.

Strategy #2: Bull Put Spread

This is my favourite strategy for Alphabet (short a Put and long a lower strike Put). While it has limited upside potential, it comes with significant leverage with the price in a certain range. Besides, this is not a very complicated strategy. Think about a short Put and just add some extra protection and leverage (a short Put in a margin account brings indeed high leverage but is actually more complex than a Put Spread!).

In my initial article, I tried to describe potential outcomes in different scenarios. But let’s see what actually happened, like I posted through different comments on my articles. In these six months we’ve been through almost all those scenarios:

Basically, there are two main patterns that I follow:

  • If the stock goes up, I close my Put Spreads before expiration, because they are deeply profitable, to avoid a price reversal.
  • If the stock goes down, I roll down (lower strike prices) my Put Spreads to a more distant expiration date. I sometimes add more funds here (because there is normally a more significant undervaluation with the stock down). However, as explained above, in this case I didn’t add substantially more funds because I keep my overall position at ~1x instead of 1.5x.
  • There is also a theoretical possibility that the stock goes down and I don't want to roll down. For example, let's take the 170/165 Put Sep'25 that I opened in February, with the stock at $185. In less than a month the stock went to $171.5 and I closed it for $88 loss (per contract), or a negative 25% ROI. If I consider that there is irreparable damage to the business model, I could just close it for a loss (well, in this case offset by the profit from the first iteration) and walk away. However, this is a very unlikely scenario for Alphabet, like discussed in the Risks section. Please notice that I never encounter the maximum possible loss for an iteration because I always close unprofitable Put Spreads before expiration, with some extrinsic value remained.

We can observe that, due to the power of options combined with carefully chosen moments for opening, closing and rolling down the positions, my Put Spread strategy is profitable even with the stock price actually down today (~$157) from the November level ($164.5).

Takeaway

I was interested in Alphabet due to a combination of strong economic moat (for most of their business segments) and still double-digit revenue growth (although declining). This is emphasized by 12% revenue growth in Q1'25, with Google Cloud up 28%, and several AI achievements.

Derived from a DCF model and backed by a comparison with Alphabet's five-year averages, my Fair Value estimate is $193.4, about 23% potential upside from the current price ($157), slightly lower than I estimated in November or in February, due to downward sales revisions for the next five years and an increased WACC, partially offset by a higher growth in perpetuity.

Risks include trade war (with possible lower demand for ads or for Google Cloud, or possible retaliation from Europe), DOJ accusations (although it's unlikely that all their proposals will be implemented) and Google Search competition. However, even in a pessimistic scenario including a serious market share loss for Google Search, Fair Value would be $165, very slightly above the current price.

While my overall exposure dropped from a 1.5x position in November to 1x today due to increased risks, my strategy is twofold:

  • a stock position, and I trimmed my position when the price went above my Fair Value.
  • a bull Put Spread position with options, which is proven to be a profitable strategy for a stagnant or even slightly decreasing stock price. With the stock up, I close the deeply profitable Put Spread, with the stock down I roll down the Put Spread to a more distant expiration date.

data.title: In The End, President Trump Was Right... Wasn't He?
data.description:
The stock market is recovering lost ground and the S&P 500 (SP500) is finally back to positive YTD returns. Liberation Day is still fresh in our minds, but more than a month has passed since then and much has changed. In addition to suspending retaliatory tariffs, President Trump has managed to strike a favorable deal with the UK, and trade tensions with China are no longer as worrying as before. Europe has so far taken a wait-and-see approach and is keen to reach an agreement with the U.S. The tariffs were widely criticized from the outset, but was President Trump right in the end?

At first glance, it may seem that the U.S. economy has emerged stronger from this situation. Everything is as it was before, except that there are new tariffs that will bring billions of dollars into the U.S. coffers. In addition, CPI data below expectations has caught many investors off guard: what if the tariffs do not generate inflation as many believe? What if President Trump is right in wanting lower interest rates?

In recent days, new scenarios have been emerging, and in this article I will explain my point of view on the matter.

The main goal is still too far away

As for inflation, I was surprised by the lower-than-expected result, which gives me hope for the future. However, we cannot completely rule out a scenario in which inflation will rise this year. Although much lower than they could have been, tariffs are in place and may gradually push prices up. I will never tire of saying this, but tariffs are not paid by the countries that are subject to them, but by the end consumers in the importing country. Companies were smart to fill their inventories before the tariffs took effect, which has helped them weather this period of high uncertainty and avoid raising prices.

So, a scenario of rising inflation is still possible, albeit less likely than a few weeks ago.

In fact, it is almost certain that rates will remain at current levels at the June meeting: it will take some time before there is another cut. If inflation were completely defeated, the Fed would have no reason not to cut rates.

Clearly, the issue of inflation is much less alarming now that tariffs have been significantly reduced. With triple-digit import tariffs on China, the U.S. economy was heading towards stagflation; this is no longer the case. I believe that inflation may even rise, but not to 2022 levels.

According to the Peterson Institute for International Economics, a 10% universal tariff could bring $2.53 trillion into the U.S. economy between 2025 and 2034. Not bad, especially if they have minimal impact on inflation.

However, tariffs also have negative effects on both companies and households, which is why the net total falls to $1.57 trillion from 2025 to 2034. This is still a fairly high figure, but it is very challenging to estimate the impact that a 10% universal tariff will have on households and businesses. It could be much better or much worse than that. Incidentally, the higher the tariffs, the lower the net total. Perhaps it would have been better not to include tariffs at all?

As of today, the consumer confidence index is at the same levels as at the peak of the pandemic, and this decline began in January 2025, then worsened when tariffs became the main focus.

The same applies to the financial situation of households, where the gap between "good" and "bad" has narrowed considerably.

In any case, the real problem for the U.S. is not tariffs, but bringing down interest rates at the long end of the yield curve.

Trillions and trillions of dollars must be refinanced over the next three years, and even a 1% difference can make a huge difference in future government interest payments. The purpose of the tariffs is clearly to increase government revenue, but if spending is not kept in check, no tariff can fix the U.S. deficit.

Since the Trump administration took office, the yield on 10-year T-Bonds (US10Y) has improved by only 11 basis points, too little to make the debt more sustainable. Yet there had been significant improvements during the first few weeks. At the beginning of March, the yield on 10-year T-Bonds had fallen to 4.10%, and after Liberation Day it had even fallen to 3.86%. When China responded with retaliatory tariffs, the bond vigilantes forced the Trump administration to take a less aggressive approach to tariffs, as the yield on 10-year T-bonds shot up 70 basis points in a week.

Since then, there has been a de-escalation, but the yield on 10-year T-Bonds is not far from its April 11 high. Bond vigilantes are still not convinced that universal tariffs of 10% (30% on China) are the right approach to make the U.S. deficit more sustainable. Yet, the stock market is a runaway train that shows no signs of slowing down.

The tariffs could indeed lead to trillions of dollars in revenue, but it is clear that the bond market is not experiencing a moment of optimism. Debt sustainability is worrying bond vigilantes, especially at the long end of the curve. What if the costs of the tariffs ultimately exceed the revenues?

Due to high rates, net interest costs are expected to rise sharply until 2035. Initial estimates suggest gross revenue from tariffs of $253 billion per year (with a universal tariff of 10%), but interest could cost $1.38 trillion per year until 2035. In addition to revenue, optimizing spending may be more important in reducing the debt burden.

So far, during FY2025, net interest expenses have reached second place. This is money lost, because it could have been spent on improving healthcare, education, and anything else that would have benefited the country. Instead, at this rate, interest is likely to eat up more and more of the government's revenue. Logically, every country pays interest to finance itself, but when it is the second largest expense, something is wrong.

Furthermore, not only will trillions of dollars be refinanced at a higher rate, but the fiscal deficit continues to grow year on year.

So, returning to the headline, was President Trump right about tariffs?

At least so far, he has been right about inflation, with CPI data showing the lowest level in four years. Economists may have been overly alarmist, but we need a few more months to confirm that the tariffs have not impacted inflation. In fact, for the time being, the Fed does not appear to be planning to lower rates, and consumer confidence has deteriorated.

As for the issue of debt sustainability, tariffs have created uncertainty, alarming bond vigilantes. Since the end of January, the 10-year T-Bond Yield has remained quite high. Before the escalation of the trade war, there had been improvements; today, even though there is less tension, the long end of the yield curve remains problematic. For it to fall again, U.S. debt needs to be perceived as safer, so a reduction in the deficit is urgently needed.

However, the exact opposite is happening, and frankly I don't think tariff revenues will do much to change the situation. In fact, they were the cause of the April 11 spike in the 10-year T-Bond Yield.

Finally, the risk of recession has fallen sharply in a matter of weeks, but remains much higher than in January 2025. The stock market is celebrating the de-escalation with China, but wouldn't it have been better if there had been no escalation in the first place? Creating a problem and then solving it ultimately brings you back to square one.

data.title: UnitedHealth Group: Capitulation After A 50% Plunge, I Am A Buyer
data.description:
UnitedHealth Group (NYSE:UNH) has been in the news a lot over the past several months, and the news has not been good. Back in December 2024, the CEO was shot and killed and that event seemed to cause the stock to drop from just over $600 per share, down to around the $465 level. The stock was then stuck in a trading range for the past few months, but in April, the share price actually crossed back over the $600 level, and it seemed like investor confidence was returning back to the stock. During the past few weeks when investors were fearing the worst over tariffs and the potential for a recession, UnitedHealth Group shares seemed like a great place to seek safety from the risks of tariffs and a possible recession. This seems to have been why the stock was able to rebound back over $600 per share. However, the stock started sliding again soon after it hit $600, and as tariff and recession fears faded it has trended lower for the past month or so. Then on May 13, the stock plunged further when the company announced the recently hired replacement CEO had decided to leave the company for personal reasons and the company also withdrew guidance for 2025. Whenever I see what appears to be a high quality stock plunge as this one has, it makes me want to take a closer look to evaluate if there is a buying opportunity. With this in mind, let's take a closer look.

The Chart

As the chart below shows, this stock has dropped by nearly 50% from the recent highs, and it has done so in a very short time. The shares are now trading well below the 200-day moving average which is around $535, and also below the 50-day moving average which is around $479. This stock is now deeply oversold with the Relative Strength Index or "RSI" at just around 15. It is rare to see stocks get this oversold and it appears to be a capitulation-like sell off. The volume of more than 59 million shares traded on May 13, was heavy and could also be another sign of capitulation.

Reasons For The Plunge

The primary reasons for the plunge appear to be the May 13th announcement from the company stating that the CEO was suddenly resigning and also because the company withdrew guidance due to higher than expected medical expenses. I have to admit, the combination of the CEO resigning (even if it is said to be for personal reasons) and the withdrawing of guidance does make me wonder if more bad news is coming and perhaps that had something to do with the CEO resigning. On the other hand, when you consider the fate of the last CEO and the potential ongoing threats and the animosity that some people seem to have for healthcare CEO's, as well as all the other government scrutiny, I can see why some executives would just think they are better off being CEO at a company that is not so controversial or potentially risky.

Around May 7, UnitedHealth Group was sued by investors that claimed it hid the business impact of the CEO being killed in December and that it had changed strategies leading to higher claim denials, amongst other issues. I think this class-action lawsuit might be expanded after the company announced that the CEO was departing after such a short tenure. As if this weren't enough, the Department of Justice "DOJ" is investigating the company over its Medicare billing policies. While this isn't good news, we know that the DOJ is investigating many large publicly traded companies and this includes Alphabet (GOOGL), (GOOG).

I know that some investors are viewing the recent news flow from this company and the CEO departure as a red flag, and this should not be ignored since there could be more bad news to come and the CEO may have departed suddenly for more than just personal reasons. However, there is a good chance the CEO did leave for personal or health reasons, or out of concern for his safety or that of his family. Therefore, I am not going to assume the worst and I feel that at this much lower valuation, I am possibly "getting paid" to take on these potential risks. I also think there is a good chance that based on the enormous decline of nearly 50% in the share price, and based on the heavy volume that looks like capitulation, the plunge in the stock is way overdone. Let's face it, after the highly negative news flow over the past several months, many investors are probably sick of the seemingly endless flow of bad news and they wanted to get out of this stock after multiple disappointments.

Reasons For Optimism

I see a number of reasons for optimism, first of which is valuation. This stock is now trading for just a bit over 10 times earnings estimates, and so even if estimates are reduced in the coming quarters, the PE multiple is still likely to be way below the market average which is currently around 27 for the S&P 500 Index (SPY).

I believe that the company withdrawing guidance for 2025 will put some investors off for the rest of this year, but I do believe that once guidance is re-established (potentially in 2026), this will help lift the stock and allow for multiple expansion.

I also believe higher Medicare rates and other rate plan increases will fuel a return to growth for 2026. This could also fuel significant expansion in the price to earnings multiple.

The fact that Andrew Witty stepped down so suddenly from the CEO position is definitely alarming to many investors; however, I see these concerns being overblown and at least partially offset because a longtime executive and former CEO of UnitedHealth named Stephen Hemsley is taking up the CEO role once again. Mr. Hemsley served as CEO from 2006 to 2017, and from what I have seen he is well-regarded. Plus, the share price of UnitedHealth did extremely well under his tenure. The share price was roughly $50 in 2006 and around $150 in 2017, so it roughly tripled during his term. A triple in 11 years, is a return that many investors would be very pleased with.

Any slightly positive news announcement or even an earnings report that is close to meeting analyst estimates could be enough to spark a big rebound in the share price. Short sellers might add to the buying pressure when some good news comes out or even if just no more bad news comes out. It might be a while, but settling shareholder lawsuits will also be welcome news.

The Balance Sheet and Earnings Estimates

UnitedHealth is the largest health insurance company in the U.S. and it has revenue estimates of roughly $450 billion for 2025. As for the balance sheet, it has just over $81 billion in debt and around $34 billion in cash. I view the balance sheet as very solid considering the significant cash balances along with the consistent cash flow and stability of the insurance industry.

As shown below, analysts expect the company to earn about $26 per share in 2025, and while I would not put a lot of faith in this number, it implies a price to earnings ratio of just over 11 times earnings. This means that even if earnings come in below estimates, this stock will still be trading at a way below the market multiple. I personally would assume that actual earnings might be 5-10% below the analysts estimates because they have not had a chance to revise these numbers lower in some cases. However, I am more optimistic that the company can meet the estimates for 2026 and 2027, and this is based on expense reductions and share buybacks that the company has been making and also on rate hikes that are planned going forward.

Share Buybacks And Dividends

UnitedHealth has a history of share buybacks as well as paying dividends. During Q1 of 2025, the company returned nearly $5 billion to shareholders through share buybacks and dividend payments. UnitedHealth currently pays a quarterly dividend of $2.10 per share, which totals $8.40 per share annually. This provides a yield of about 2.2%. This company has been growing the dividend rapidly which makes this stock ideal for dividend growth investors. For example, in 2020, the company paid $4.83 per share in dividends, but thanks to strong increases in the payout each year, the dividend has nearly doubled to the current $8.40 per share in total annual dividends. With the payout ratio at just around 30%, the dividend appears very secure and there is plenty of room for additional increases.

Where I Could Be Wrong

There is a chance that Mr. Witty resigned because he sees problems that will not be resolved easily or favorably for this company. However, I find it encouraging that Stephen Hemsey is stepping back into the CEO role and it seems unlikely to me that he would do this if he did not believe in a favorable outcome for this company going forward. I do have some concerns about the DOJ investigations into Medicare billing practices, but I think this will most likely be resolved with a fine or one time charge, if a serious issue even comes up. A UnitedHealth subsidiary (Change Healthcare) was the target of a cyberattack in 2024, and it or the parent company could be targeted again, which could result in additional expenses.

My Buying Strategy

I am not expecting a big short-term rebound in this stock, although it certainly could happen since the stock is now so oversold. My strategy is to accumulate shares over time and build a full position slowly. I might also sell call options on part of my position, or sell put options in order to take advantage of the generous premiums that UnitedHealth stock options currently offer.

In Summary

There is a lot of "hair" on this stock so this won't be a large position for me. I view the challenges I see for this company as short-term in nature and resolvable, and I also think that most companies hit some type of rough patch now and then. UnitedHealth has had many good years up until recently which makes me think this series of recent issues is an anomaly. Health insurance is a basic need for most people and history shows that a properly managed company can make a lot of money handling policy rates and claims. With a PE ratio that is way below the market average and after a nearly 50% haircut in the share price, a lot of bad news is priced into the stock, perhaps overly so. I think it makes sense to buy some shares now and build a full (but relatively small) position and forget about it for a while. I also like that this company appears to be recession-resistant. It could take time to resolve the issues that are weighing on the stock now, but the upside potential appears to be significant.
